policy_statement: Create a Quality Assurance Verifier position to support the guidelines and applicability of the Building Performance Ordinance.
mechanism_description: Establishing a QA verifier strengthens implementation of a building performance ordinance, improving compliance and follow-through so building operations and upgrades reduce energy use and associated emissions.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Performance standards/benchmarking/retuning/retrofits reduce energy input required to deliver the same building services (heating/cooling/lighting), i.e., improved energy productivity (same service → less energy).
primary_causal_pathway: Building operations/retrofits mandated → ↓ energy demand (electricity + on-site fuels) → ↓ combustion & grid generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human/system activity: Require large existing (and sometimes new) buildings to measure and disclose energy use (benchmark), tune/optimize controls (retune), and/or meet minimum performance targets that trigger retrofits and operational improvements.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Improved HVAC control, envelope performance, equipment efficiency, and reduced waste heat lowers delivered energy needed to maintain indoor comfort and provide lighting/plug loads.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced electricity consumption and reduced on-site fossil fuel use (e.g., natural gas for space/water heating) lowers CO₂ (and to a lesser extent CH₄/N₂O) emissions from the building sector and associated energy supply.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is lowering operational building energy consumption, which reduces upstream greenhouse gas emissions from electricity generation and on-site fuel combustion.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s central lever is reducing operational energy use in buildings, which directly reduces GHG emissions from electricity generation and on-site fuel combustion (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While better-performing buildings can incidentally improve comfort and sometimes resilience (e.g., during heat), those effects are not the mechanism’s primary or universal climate-relevant outcome; the consistent intended and realized effect across instances is emissions reduction via energy reduction.  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary:  
   - Resource Efficiency: By definition, performance standards/benchmarking/retuning/retrofits reduce energy inputs for the same service, making energy use more efficient in all implementations.  
4) Edge cases applied: Even when paired with incentives or phased future standards (e.g., “beginning in 2030”), the core mechanism still operates through energy-use reduction to cut emissions.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy that is truly a building performance ordinance/benchmarking-retuning-retrofit requirement necessarily targets operational energy reduction; thus Mitigation (and inherent Resource Efficiency) applies across cities/contexts.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some building performance ordinances may allow compliance via fuel-switching/electrification or procurement of clean energy/credits; the dominant effect remains mitigation, but if a specific policy variant primarily drives decarbonization without reducing energy (rare), it would be better represented by a different mechanism (e.g., building_electrification → fuel_switching).
key_indicators: "Create a Quality Assurance Verifier position"; "support the guidelines and applicability"; "Building Performance Ordinance"
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: improved ordinance compliance, better data quality, faster identification of operational issues, possible improved indoor comfort and heat resilience through better building operations—however not explicitly directed at local hazards.
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument could be interpreted as a regulatory/administrative enforcement measure tied to a mandate, but the text focuses on creating a position (capacity/implementation support) rather than imposing a new requirement on regulated entities.
